rawText:
If you've read through the posts leading up to this one, you know that a Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) policy is essential for protecting a brand's market positioning, profitability, and long-term growth. A well-designed MAP policy helps brands maintain consistent pricing across various sales channels, ensuring that their products are not devalued through excessive discounting. However, even the best MAP policies and enforcement strategies can fail without the right data. Accurate and timely data plays a critical role in enforcing MAP compliance, identifying violations, and taking corrective actions. In this post, we'll explore why data is the backbone of MAP compliance, how it impacts decision-making, and the challenges brands face when data is incomplete or inaccurate. We all know that the e-commerce landscape is vast, with seemingly countless retailers and marketplaces, and an increasing number of third-party sellers popping up by the hundreds each day. Brands that we talk to at Omnitok are admittedly struggling to not only understand the ever-changing market dynamics, but they want to be successful in spite of the complexities that arise daily. Better said, they don't just want to tread water, they want to swim like Michael Phelps or Bobby Finke. Putting it bluntly, manually monitoring this expansive sea of retailers to identify MAP violations isn't just impractical, it's borderline crazy. There are simply too many sites for a team of humans to monitor, let alone to complete the necessary data validation, which is critical to any MAP program's success. Automated MAP compliance tools rely on consistently extracted data to track advertised prices across retailers, marketplaces, and websites so that teams of people don't have to. But - and let me be very clear about this - all data extractors are not created equal, and the data accuracy spectrum is wide, not to mention sometimes scary. The Role of Monitoring & Scraping Frequency Constant monitoring helps brands detect MAP violations quickly, allowing them to act before unauthorized pricing has a chance to hurt their brand. The faster a violation is identified, the sooner a brand can address the issue with the retailer, protecting its pricing integrity. To be clear, this doesn't mean that brands should expect real-time monitoring or "always-on" monitoring. That sort of constant presence on a retailer site is the best way to get blocked and lose your channel visibility for good. Brands are best served by a solution provider that will balance scraping frequency with your team's bandwidth and unique requirements, so as not to be detected by the retailer/marketplace. Important Note: Remaining undetected is the first step in capturing accurate data. Enforcing Compliance Consistently Accurate data ensures that brands can enforce their MAP policies consistently across all retail partners. When data is incomplete or outdated, enforcement becomes selective, leading to potential friction with retail partners and damaging relationships. Inconsistent enforcement can also open a brand up to accusations of favoritism, making it harder to maintain strong partnerships. With accurate data, brands can hold all retailers to the same standard, ensuring fairness and transparency. Maintaining Brand Value and Customer Trust Pricing discrepancies across different sales channels can confuse customers and erode brand trust. If a customer sees the same product advertised at vastly different prices on multiple websites, they may question the quality or authenticity of the product. Additionally, customers who purchase at a higher price may feel deceived if they later find the same product being advertised at a lower price by another retailer. Accurate data helps brands maintain pricing consistency, which protects their brand equity and ensures that customers can trust the prices they see across different platforms.

The Challenges of Managing MAP Compliance

Without Accurate Data Manual Monitoring Some brands still rely on manual monitoring to check advertised prices across sales channels. This approach is incredibly time-consuming, costly, and prone to human error. With the sheer volume of products and sellers in the e-commerce space, manual monitoring is neither scalable nor reliable. Over the last several years, we've met brands that leverage large, multi-unit teams (legal, sales, marketing) to try and accomplish the task of manually monitoring their brands. One consumer electronics customer estimated they were spending more than 500 employee hours a week to cover less than 20% of their catalog. This sort of resource strain hurts morale, productivity, sales and profits, and is simply not sustainable. Without the right data and automation tools, brands can easily miss violations and spend more time on low-value tasks than those that move the business forward. Fragmented Data E-commerce is, by nature, highly fragmented, with products being sold on multiple platforms, marketplaces, and retailer websites. Each platform has its own pricing dynamics, and many brands struggle to consolidate and normalize data from the various corners of the web. Accurate, consolidated data from all platforms is essential for effective MAP compliance. Relying on inaccurate data can lead to missed opportunities for brands to correct pricing issues before they escalate. If a violation goes undetected for too long, it can result in significant damage to a brand's reputation and financial performance. One example of this is the US holiday season, where we've seen as much as a 30% increase in MAP violations, as retailers aim to finish the year strong by capturing feverish shoppers hoping to get a bargain in a hurry.

The Financial Impact of Inaccurate Data Revenue Loss Across the Board

When MAP violations are missed due to inaccurate data, it can impact the business in several ways. Most brands will see some form of revenue loss, whether it's from missing out on sales directly from the brand's site, missed Buy Box opportunities on Amazon, or even lower account values as retailers and distributors reduce purchase orders because the expected sales velocity doesn't match reality. For the past 9 years, brands have told us that this sort of revenue loss can impact the business by as much as 5% across the board, and up to 30% for on-MAP products.

Higher Cost of Enforcement

Another financial impact brands feel when data is fragmented, inaccurate, or missing altogether is the need to allocate more resources on enforcement efforts. This comes in the way of personnel costs and includes the hours spent manually tracking violations, investigating complaints, and handling disputes with retailers. Brands that lack automated MAP monitoring tools may spend as much as 3-4 times more on enforcement compared to brands that leverage accurate data and automation. How AI-Driven MAP Solutions Protect Brand Value AI-driven MAP solutions like Omnitok's allow brands to feel confident that they are seeing the whole picture when it comes to protecting their pricing integrity. By leveraging accurate, comprehensive data, brands can detect violations early, enforce policies consistently, and maintain strong relationships with retail partners.

Challenges and Solutions in Implementing MAP Policies Monitoring and Enforcement

One of the primary challenges in implementing MAP policies is the ongoing monitoring and enforcement required to ensure compliance. With the proliferation of online retail channels, keeping track of advertised prices can be daunting. As much as brands need the pricing data to successfully drive their business, retailers equally want to keep a competitive advantage against other retailers who may be scraping their prices. We'll talk about this in our next post, but many retailers implement blocking technologies that purposely make price extraction difficult. Brands can address this challenge by investing in automated monitoring tools like Omnitok's MAP solution that couples artificial intelligence into a comprehensive scan of various first and third-party sites/marketplaces to identify price violations. There are many MAP providers out there and the most reputable will all offer similar features and functions, coupling clear evidence of violations with workflow, allowing brands to take prompt action. The best-in-class offerings will do so with upwards of 90% accuracy. At Omnitok, we pride ourselves on delivering data every day with more than 99% accuracy. Legal Considerations While MAP policies are generally legal, they must be carefully structured to comply with antitrust laws. We talked about this in a previous post, but we strongly recommend that brands consult with legal experts to ensure their MAP policies do not inadvertently constitute price-fixing. Properly drafted MAP policies should focus on advertised prices rather than dictating the final sale price, maintaining a clear distinction to avoid legal pitfalls. In Summary MAP policies are an essential tool for protecting a brand's value, ensuring fair competition, and safeguarding profit margins. By maintaining consistent pricing across all online retail channels, brands can enhance their market positioning, build stronger relationships with retailers, and gain the trust of consumers. While there are challenges in implementing and enforcing MAP policies, the benefits far outweigh the difficulties. Brands that commit to robust MAP compliance can expect to see long-term gains in both their brand equity and bottom line. Talk to our team at Omnitok today to explore how our AI-driven MAP solution can help your team remove the complexity from your MAP monitoring and enforcement.

rawText:
In our first two posts, we covered the basics of MAP programs and compliance as well as some of the common misconceptions around MAP programs. If you missed those posts, there may be value in going back to them for a refresher. In this post, we're going to examine some of the legal aspects that brands should consider when implementing a MAP (Minimum Advertised Price) policy. MAP policies can be a valuable tool for brands to maintain pricing integrity and protect their market positioning while honoring relationships with retailers of all sizes. However, implementing MAP policies requires careful navigation of legal frameworks to avoid some potentially damaging pitfalls and today we'll cover some of the most common. We know talking about legal issues can raise those tiny hairs on the back of your neck, but don't fret. MAP policies - when structured and enforced correctly - are generally beneficial and very legal. When they go awry, it's typically because the brand didn't do the necessary diligence to craft its policy with a clear understanding of antitrust laws and other regulatory requirements. Come along with us as we explore the key legal considerations that brands must consider when implementing a MAP policy. Understanding MAP Policies and the Law MAP Policies vs. Price-Fixing One of the most critical distinctions brands must understand is the difference between MAP policies and price-fixing. If this concept is new to you, Price-fixing is when a manufacturer dictates the final sale price of a product; think about the final price a consumer pays at checkout, after any promotion or sale price. This final price is executed at the discretion of the retailer and for any brand or manufacturer to dictate this price is generally illegal under antitrust laws in many jurisdictions, including the United States. However, MAP policies differ because they focus on the advertised price, not the actual selling price. Let's use a hypothetical example to illustrate this best. For the sake of this lesson, assume that I'm a brand that manufactures dripless water bowls for messy dogs. My company, Atomic Dogs, sells to the retailer PetDepot and has a MAP policy in place that prohibits my retail partners, including PetDepot, from advertising these bowls for less than $19.

99. Generally, PetDepot is considered a valuable partner and has a history of honoring my MAP prices. For their Memorial Day sale, PetDepot is offering a 15% off promo code that applies at checkout to any purchase between $100 and $500. When the customer adds an Atomic Dog bowl to their cart, meets the promotional requirement, and applies the promo code, the bowls normally listed at $19.99 can be purchased at $16.

99. Because this promotion was not specific to the Atomic Dog bowls, PetDepot is not in violation of my MAP policy. This practice is very common and the hypothetical retailer, PetDepot in this case, is not violating the brand's MAP policy. However, if Atomic Dog were to offer PetDepot a special rebate for this promotion, both parties would be skirting close to the edge of the law. MAP policies are legal as long as they are implemented unilaterally by the manufacturer. This means that the manufacturer sets the advertised price guidelines without requiring retailers to agree to them as a condition of doing business. Retailers are free to sell the product at any price they choose, but they must comply with the advertised price limits set by the MAP policy.

The Sherman Antitrust Act

In the United States, MAP policies must comply with the Sherman Antitrust Act, which prohibits agreements that unreasonably restrain trade. MAP policies that cross the line into price-fixing or involve collusion between manufacturers and retailers could be seen as violating the Sherman Act and are considered criminal acts in the US. Collusion and price-fixing agreements are considered criminal violations and can be prosecuted by the Department of Justice (DOJ). Penalties can include up to 10 years in jail and $100 million per violation. Therefore, it is essential that MAP policies are unilaterally established by the manufacturer and not the result of behind-the-scenes negotiations or agreements with retailers. Crafting a Legally Sound MAP Policy Unilateral Implementation To ensure compliance with antitrust laws and avoid fines and/or jail time, brands must implement their MAP policies unilaterally. This means that the manufacturer sets the MAP guidelines on its own, without requiring retailers to sign an agreement or commit to following the policy as a condition of selling the product. The policy should be communicated to all retailers, but it should not involve any formal agreement or contract that could be interpreted as price-fixing.

Clear and Transparent Language

The best way to avoid any confusion with retailers around your MAP policy is to ensure you're using clear and transparent language in your policy. It should explicitly state that it only applies to advertised prices, not the final sale price. It should also outline the consequences of non-compliance in a straightforward manner, such as a reduction in product allocation or termination of the retailer's authorized status. Avoid using language that could be construed as coercive or suggest that the policy is a condition of doing business. Consistent Enforcement As we mentioned in our Introduction to MAP Compliance blog, legal scrutiny can arise if a brand enforces its MAP policy inconsistently. To avoid accusations of favoritism or anti-competitive behavior, brands must apply their MAP policy uniformly across all retailers. This means taking the same enforcement actions against all violators, regardless of their size or importance to the brand. MAP policies are not meant to be subjective or left up to interpretation, especially by those in charge of the day-to-day enforcement. Consistent enforcement helps demonstrate that the policy is being applied fairly and is not a tool for manipulating the market. One best practice is to remove any internal parties who may have a vested interest in looking the other way or not uniformly enforcing violations. The team at Omnitok has rich experience in this and can offer some additional best practices to help you enforce without bias.

Avoiding Collusion and Vertical Agreements Avoid Collaborative Agreements

One of the key legal risks associated with MAP policies is the potential for them to be seen as collaborative agreements between manufacturers and retailers, which can violate antitrust laws. To mitigate this risk, brands must avoid any appearance of collusion or agreement. Retailers should not be involved in the development of the MAP policy, and there should be no negotiation over its terms. The policy should be presented as a unilateral decision by the manufacturer without any consideration for your key retail partners or channels. Be Cautious with Incentives Offering incentives or rewards to retailers for complying with a MAP policy can also raise legal concerns. While it's important to maintain good relationships with retail partners, incentives that are tied directly to MAP compliance can be viewed as a form of coercion or an agreement. Instead, focus on providing general support, such as marketing assistance or training that is not directly linked to MAP compliance.

International Considerations Varying Legal Frameworks

If your brand operates in multiple countries, it's important to recognize that the legal landscape for MAP policies can vary significantly from one jurisdiction to another. In the European Union, for example, restrictions on retail pricing are more tightly regulated under competition laws. What is permissible in the U.S. may not be allowed in the EU or other regions, so brands must tailor their MAP policies to comply with local laws. Consulting Legal Experts Given the complexity of international laws, it's advisable to consult with local legal experts in each region where your brand operates. Legal counsel can help ensure that your MAP policy complies with local regulations and provide guidance on how to implement and enforce the policy in different markets.

Legal Risks and

How to Mitigate Them Monitoring and Documentation To protect your brand from legal challenges, it's important to establish a system for monitoring MAP compliance and documenting all enforcement actions. For most brands, this includes working with a partner like Omnitok that can monitor, maintain and facilitate the communication with retailers who have violated your MAP policy. Having a partner to relieve the burden of monitoring is key because the onus is on brands to produce documentation of policy violations, and to keep track of the steps taken to address non-compliance. In addition to working with a third party, having a well-documented process can be invaluable if your brand faces legal scrutiny or challenges from retailers. Legal battles add up and brands can incur hundreds of thousands of dollars in legal costs to fight a single retailer. Staying on top of your retailers' published prices is going to be the most important piece of a well-executed MAP policy.

Educating Retail Partners

While MAP policies must be implemented unilaterally, an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure. As such, it's vital to educate - and overcommunicate to - your retail partners about your policy and its purpose. This can help prevent misunderstandings and reduce the likelihood of disputes. However, be cautious in how you communicate this information—focus on providing unbiased education rather than seeking agreements or commitments. Taking the time to be thorough, patient and impartial in your communication will likely result in a deeper, more meaningful relationship with your retailers.

Regular Policy Reviews

If you've made it this far in our post, we appreciate your curiosity and interest in Omnitok's guidance. However, while we have a wealth of experience to offer customers, you should not take this post as legal guidance. Laws and market conditions change over time, so it's essential to regularly review and update your MAP policy to ensure continued compliance. Periodic reviews with your legal advisors can help identify potential legal risks and allow you to adjust your policy to reflect new regulations or market dynamics. Conclusion Here at Omnitok, we pride ourselves on being good partners, listening closely to our customers' needs and doing all we can to empower brands every day. We're rooting for you as you work to implement your MAP policy, using a careful balance between protecting your brand's pricing integrity and navigating complex legal frameworks. By understanding the legal considerations, crafting a clear and transparent policy, and enforcing it consistently, brands can effectively use MAP policies to safeguard their value while minimizing legal risks. If you are interested in learning more about how Omnitok's AI-powered MAP solution can help, please reach out. We're here to help.

rawText:
In our last post, we talked about enforcing a MAP policy and some of the legal considerations that brands must take to ensure that a MAP policy is legal and that you're setting yourself up for long-term success. Today we'll take one step back and talk about how a well-structured policy can make your life easier and protect the business financially. Retail is moving faster today than it ever has. Brands are facing constant pressure to maintain their market position and profitability. One of the most effective strategies to achieve this is through the implementation of Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) policies. These policies not only protect the perceived value of a brand but also safeguard its bottom line. This blog post will delve into the ways MAP policies contribute to brand protection and financial health, and why they are a critical tool for any brand looking to thrive in today's market. Preserving Brand Value Maintaining Price Integrity MAP policies help maintain the integrity of a brand's pricing structure. When all retailers adhere to a minimum advertised price, it prevents price erosion that occurs from aggressive discounting. Consistent pricing reinforces the brand's value proposition, ensuring customers perceive the products as worth the investment. This is particularly important for premium and luxury brands, where price is closely tied to perceived quality. Enhancing Brand Perception Consumers often equate higher prices with higher quality. By enforcing MAP policies, brands can avoid the downward spiral of price competition (aka "race to the bottom") that can tarnish their image. Consistent pricing across retail channels ensures that the brand is seen as stable and reliable, enhancing overall brand perception. This consistency helps build long-term customer loyalty, as consumers come to trust that they are getting a quality product regardless of where they purchase it.

Ensuring Fair Competition Leveling the Playing Field

When executed well, MAP policies create a level playing field for all retailers, from large chains to small independent stores. Without MAP policies, larger retailers might engage in price wars, using their buying power to undercut smaller competitors. This can drive smaller retailers out of the market, reducing consumer choice and ultimately harming the brand's distribution network. By holding all retailers to the same advertised prices, brands can support a healthy and diverse retail ecosystem.

Protecting Retailer Margins

For retailers, MAP policies are beneficial because they help protect profit margins. In a market without MAP enforcement, retailers can feel pressured to continuously lower prices to stay competitive, eroding their margins and profitability. MAP policies provide a stable pricing environment, allowing retailers to focus on customer service and experience rather than engaging in destructive price competition. This leads to better business relationships and a more sustainable retail environment. Strengthening Retailer Relationships Building Trust with Retailers Your relationship with your retail partners is best when it's based on mutual trust. Implementing and enforcing MAP policies can significantly strengthen relationships with retail partners who typically appreciate the stability and support that comes from a brand being committed to maintaining price integrity. This trust can translate into increased loyalty, where retailers are more likely to prioritize and promote products from brands that enforce MAP policies. In turn, brands benefit from enhanced product placement and dedicated promotional efforts from their retail partners.

Encouraging Retailer Investment

When retailers are confident that they will not be undercut by competitors, they are more willing to invest in stocking, promoting, and selling a brand's products. This can include investing in better shelf space, in-store displays, and marketing efforts. These types of investments enhance the brand's visibility and attractiveness to consumers, driving sales and reinforcing the brand's market position.

Protecting Profit Margins Preventing Price Wars

One of the most direct benefits of MAP policies is the prevention of price wars. In the absence of MAP enforcement, retailers might engage in aggressive discounting to attract customers, pricing solely to beat the competition and sacrificing profit margins in the process. This not only hurts their bottom line but can also devalue the brand in the eyes of consumers. By establishing a minimum advertised price, brands can prevent such destructive price competition and protect their profitability. Predictability Is Key Well-crafted MAP policies help stabilize market prices, which is crucial for long-term financial planning and strategy. When prices fluctuate wildly, it becomes difficult for brands to forecast revenue, manage inventory, and make informed business decisions. Stable pricing allows for more accurate financial projections Enhancing Consumer Trust Consistent Pricing As consumers, we tend to place a high value on consistency. When they see the same price for a product across different retailers, it reinforces their trust in the brand. This consistency assures us that we are getting a fair deal, no matter where we choose to shop. It also reduces the likelihood of feeling cheated or dissatisfied, which can show up as that sinking feeling in the stomach when we find significantly lower prices elsewhere after making a purchase. Reducing Fraud and Counterfeits MAP policies can also help reduce the risk of fraud and counterfeit products. When prices vary widely, it can be a red flag for counterfeit goods, especially in online marketplaces. Consistent pricing makes it easier for consumers to identify legitimate products and authorized sellers, thereby reducing the chances of falling victim to fraud. This protection enhances the overall trust in the brand and its products.

Challenges and Solutions in Implementing MAP Policies Monitoring and Enforcement

One of the primary challenges in implementing MAP policies is the ongoing monitoring and enforcement required to ensure compliance. With the proliferation of online retail channels, keeping track of advertised prices can be daunting. As much as brands need the pricing data to successfully drive their business, retailers equally want to keep a competitive advantage against other retailers who may be scraping their prices. We'll talk about this in our next post, but many retailers implement blocking technologies that purposely make price extraction difficult. Brands can address this challenge by investing in automated monitoring tools like Omnitok's MAP solution that couples artificial intelligence into a comprehensive scan of various first and third-party sites/marketplaces to identify price violations. There are many MAP providers out there and the most reputable will all offer similar features and functions, coupling clear evidence of violations with workflow, allowing brands to take prompt action. The best-in-class offerings will do so with upwards of 90% accuracy. At Omnitok, we pride ourselves on delivering data every day with more than 99% accuracy. Legal Considerations While MAP policies are generally legal, they must be carefully structured to comply with antitrust laws. We talked about this in a previous post, but we strongly recommend that brands consult with legal experts to ensure their MAP policies do not inadvertently constitute price-fixing. Properly drafted MAP policies should focus on advertised prices rather than dictating the final sale price, maintaining a clear distinction to avoid legal pitfalls. In Summary MAP policies are an essential tool for protecting a brand's value, ensuring fair competition, and safeguarding profit margins. By maintaining consistent pricing across all online retail channels, brands can enhance their market positioning, build stronger relationships with retailers, and gain the trust of consumers. While there are challenges in implementing and enforcing MAP policies, the benefits far outweigh the difficulties. Brands that commit to robust MAP compliance can expect to see long-term gains in both their brand equity and bottom line. Talk to our team at Omnitok today to explore how our AI-driven MAP solution can help your team remove the complexity from your MAP monitoring and enforcement.

rawText:
Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) policies are a cornerstone of many brands' strategies to maintain pricing integrity and protect their market positioning. Despite their importance, there are numerous misconceptions about MAP compliance that can lead to misunderstandings and ineffective implementation. This post will address some of the most common misconceptions about MAP compliance, hopefully providing clarity and insight to help brands and retailers navigate this crucial aspect of retail strategy.

Misconception 1: MAP Policies Control Selling Prices

One of the most prevalent misconceptions about MAP policies is that they dictate the final selling price of a product. This is simply not true, but you may be surprised to learn that this point bears constant clarification both for retailers and internal stakeholders as well. In reality, MAP policies only regulate the minimum price at which a product can be advertised, not the price at which it is actually sold. Clarification: MAP policies are designed to prevent price erosion and maintain brand value by ensuring consistent advertised prices. Retailers are free to sell the product at any final price they choose, as long as the advertised price adheres to the MAP policy. This distinction is crucial for legal compliance and maintaining healthy relationships with retail partners. One example of this can be retailers offering a promo code that applies to all products in a shopper's cart or offering a non-advertised price on their site ("see final price in cart"). While the brand may not always agree with this approach, a MAP policy cannot prevent a retailer from discounting the final price as they see fit. Misconception 2: MAP Policies Are Illegal Some believe that MAP policies are illegal or constitute price-fixing. This misconception often stems from a misunderstanding of antitrust laws and how they apply to MAP policies. Clarification: While MAP policies are not allowed in many countries outside the US (e.g. European retailers operate off of what's known as the RRP - Recommended Retail Price - which is essentially MSRP in the states) MAP policies are generally legal in many jurisdictions, including the United States, as long as they are implemented correctly. They differ from resale price maintenance (RPM) policies, which set the actual sale price and can be subject to more stringent legal scrutiny. MAP policies should be unilaterally established by the manufacturer and focus solely on advertised prices to ensure they comply with antitrust laws.

Misconception 3: Only Large

Brands Need MAP Policies

Another common misconception is that only large, well-established brands benefit from MAP policies. This is a common misconception that has occasionally (and unethically) been used by retailers to dissuade a brand from implementing a policy altogether. Smaller brands or startups might believe that MAP policies are unnecessary or too complex for their operations, yet - ironically - it's this very belief that can often cause the brand's products to be commoditized and can even negatively affect sales right out of the gate. Clarification: MAP policies can be beneficial for brands of all sizes. For smaller brands, maintaining price integrity upon launch is critical for building a strong market presence and competing against larger players. MAP policies help ensure that products are perceived as valuable and prevent undercutting that can erode brand equity. Implementing a MAP policy early can set the foundation for sustainable growth and market stability.

Misconception 4: MAP Policies Are

Too Difficult to Enforce Some brands avoid implementing MAP policies because they believe enforcement is too challenging, especially with the proliferation of online marketplaces and price scraping blocking measures that retailers put in place to try and prevent brands from monitoring their sites. Clarification: While enforcing MAP policies can be complex, advances in technology have made it more manageable. Automated monitoring tools like Omnitok can track advertised prices across a multitude of sites and marketplaces, quickly identifying violations. Brands can also establish clear guidelines and consequences for non-compliance, removing what can sometimes be a lengthy, subjective decision-making process and making enforcement more straightforward. Consistent enforcement is essential for maintaining the policy's effectiveness and credibility. Misconception 5: MAP Policies Harm Retailer Relationships There is a belief that MAP policies can strain relationships with retailers, who may view them as restrictive or unfair. Clarification: When communicated effectively, MAP policies can actually strengthen retailer relationships. Retailers benefit from the stable pricing environment that MAP policies create, which protects their margins and allows for fair competition. Brands should engage with their retail partners, explaining the benefits of MAP compliance and working collaboratively to ensure mutual success. Transparent communication and support can turn MAP policies into a partnership tool (aka win-win) rather than a point of contention.

Misconception 6: MAP Policies Are

One-Size-Fits-All Some brands assume that once a MAP policy is set, it applies universally across all products and retailers without the need for adjustments. Clarification: MAP policies should be tailored to fit the specific needs of the brand and its market dynamics. Factors such as product categories, market conditions, and retailer types can influence the design and implementation of MAP policies. Brands may need to adjust their policies (and sometimes even prices) over time to reflect changes in the market and feedback from retail partners. A flexible approach ensures that the policy remains relevant and effective while signaling to retailers that you want to be a good partner over time.

Misconception 7: Consumers Don't Care About MAP Compliance

Another misconception is that consumers are indifferent to MAP compliance and only care about getting the lowest possible price. Clarification: While price is a significant factor in purchasing decisions, consumers also value consistency, credibility, and trust. When prices are consistent across different retailers, it builds consumer confidence in the brand and reduces confusion. Moreover, consistent pricing helps avoid situations where consumers feel misled or frustrated by significant price discrepancies which can raise red flags and force them to opt for a different product entirely. Savvy brands recognize that MAP compliance contributes to a positive consumer experience and reinforces brand loyalty, ultimately increasing the lifetime value of a customer.

Misconception 8: MAP Policies Are Only Relevant for Online Sales

Another misconception is that consumers are indifferent to MAP compliance and only care about getting the lowest possible price. Clarification: While price is a significant factor in purchasing decisions, consumers also value consistency, credibility, and trust. When prices are consistent across different retailers, it builds consumer confidence in the brand and reduces confusion. Moreover, consistent pricing helps avoid situations where consumers feel misled or frustrated by significant price discrepancies which can raise red flags and force them to opt for a different product entirely. Savvy brands recognize that MAP compliance contributes to a positive consumer experience and reinforces brand loyalty, ultimately increasing the lifetime value of a customer.

Misconception 9: MAP Policies Are a

One-Time Effort There is a misconception that once a MAP policy is established, it requires little to no ongoing effort, and nothing could be further from the truth. Clarification: Effective MAP compliance requires continuous monitoring, equitable enforcement, and occasional adjustment to either market demands or retailer sales. Brands need to regularly review their policies to ensure they are up-to-date with market trends and legal requirements. Ongoing communication with retail partners and investment in monitoring tools like Omnitok is crucial for maintaining compliance. Treating MAP policies as an ongoing process rather than a one-time effort ensures their long-term success and effectiveness. Misconception 10: MAP Policies Guarantee Sales Success Finally, some brands may assume that implementing a MAP policy will automatically lead to increased sales and market success. Clarification: At Omnitok, we are rooting for you and want to see all brands maximize sales and optimize profits. While MAP policies are an important tool for protecting brand value and maintaining pricing integrity, they are not a standalone solution or a silver bullet. Long-term success in the marketplace requires a comprehensive strategy that includes quality products, effective marketing, ongoing monitoring, strong retailer relationships, and excellent customer service. MAP policies can support these efforts by providing a stable pricing environment, but they must be part of a broader strategic plan to achieve sustained growth and profitability. Conclusion Understanding and addressing common misconceptions about MAP compliance is crucial for brands looking to implement effective pricing strategies. The creation and enforcement of a well-designed MAP policy can seem like a daunting task and will require constant supervision and review, but - when done correctly - MAP policies play a vital role in protecting brand value, ensuring fair competition, and supporting retailer relationships. By clarifying these misconceptions and adopting a proactive, flexible approach to MAP compliance, I hope we've shown that brands can leverage these policies to enhance their market positioning and achieve the long-term success you're planning for.

rawText:
In today's hyper-competitive retail landscape, how customers interact with your brand daily (aka brand perception) and how that collective experience influences the long-term value associated with your brand in the customer's mind (aka brand equity) is more important than ever. Brands are struggling to do all they can to not only win the purchase in the moment but also to win the hearts and minds of customers. One of the key strategies savvy brands are using to preserve their value and market positioning is the implementation of a Minimum Advertised Price (MAP) policy. These policies set the lowest price at which a product can be advertised, ensuring a level playing field for all retailers and protecting the brand's pricing integrity. Today, let's explore the fundamentals of MAP compliance, why it's crucial for brands, and start to outline how brands effectively implement and enforce these policies. Whether you are just starting out and considering implementing a MAP policy, or have a solid policy in place already, my hope is that the following information can be helpful to set you down the right path or to serve as a refresher, helping you get back to basics or reset your plan altogether. What is MAP Compliance? MAP compliance refers to the adherence to a brand's stipulated minimum advertised price policy. These policies are agreements between manufacturers and their authorized resellers, dictating the lowest price at which a product can be advertised. It's important to note that MAP policies do not dictate the final price at which the product can be sold, only the advertised price.

Why MAP Compliance Matters Protecting Brand Value

You've worked hard to gain brand equity with your customers and prospects and now it's time to protect those gains. One of the primary reasons brands establish MAP policies is to protect their value. When retailers engage in price wars, the value of the product can greatly diminish in the eyes of consumers. Apple, Nike, Milwaukee, Yeti, and Sonos are just a few examples of great brands that recognize the importance of a single price point for their products and it's no surprise all are considered premium brands. By maintaining a consistent advertised price, brands ensure that their products are perceived as high-quality and worth the price in the mind of the customer.

Ensuring Fair Competition

When drawn up and executed correctly, MAP policies create a level playing field for all retailers, preventing larger retailers from undercutting smaller ones. This helps to foster a diverse retail environment where smaller businesses can compete effectively without being driven out by price competition from larger chains.

Enhancing Retailer Relationships

By enforcing MAP policies consistently and judiciously, brands typically strengthen their relationships with retailers. Retailers benefit from stable pricing structures, which can lead to better margins and a more collaborative relationship with the brand. In return, brands gain loyal retail partners who are more likely to promote and prioritize their products. A common long-term effect of this leveling often leads to the brand having a better relationship with retailers of all sizes while avoiding being bullied by the top few that have the resources to undercut the competition.

Key Elements of an Effective MAP Policy Clear Communication

An effective MAP policy starts with transparent, clear and concise communication. Brands must ensure that all authorized retailers truly understand the policy, its importance, and the real-world consequences of non-compliance. This communication should be part of the onboarding process for new retailers and reinforced regularly. Consistent, Unbiased Enforcement Consistency is crucial when it comes to enforcing MAP policies. A VP of Sales in the Consumer Electronics space once told me, "we look at MAP violations like bed-time at a sleepover…everyone goes to bed at the same time, no exceptions." This is a great approach and will greatly yield results that positively impact the business over time. Brands must monitor compliance regularly and take prompt action against violators. Inconsistent monitoring, sporadic enforcement or allowing those with a conflict of interest to enforce violations can lead to confusion among retailers and undermine the policy's effectiveness. Legal Considerations Brands must ensure that their MAP policies comply with legal regulations. In the United States, MAP policies are generally legal as long as they do not fix the actual selling price and are established unilaterally by the manufacturer. However, the specifics can vary by jurisdiction, so it's essential to consult with your own legal experts when drafting and implementing MAP policies.

Implementing MAP Policies Defining the Policy

The first step in implementing a MAP policy is to clearly define it. This includes specifying the minimum advertised price for each product, outlining the scope of the policy (e.g., Does it cover all advertising channels? How is seasonality considered?), and detailing the consequences of non-compliance. Training and Education Once the policy is defined, brands must educate their retail partners. This involves providing training on the importance of MAP compliance, how to adhere to the policy, and the discussing the tools that will be used to monitor compliance. This latter is a critical step in setting retailer expectations and ensuring all parties are on the same page. Regular training sessions can help reinforce the policy and address any questions or concerns retailers may have. Monitoring Compliance Effective MAP enforcement requires continuous monitoring. Brands can use various tools and technologies to track advertised prices across different channels. Automated, intelligent monitoring solutions like Omnitok's can simplify this process by leveraging constant monitoring coupled with AI generated insights, allowing brands to quickly identify infractions, extract multichannel insights and address violations. Addressing Violations When a retailer violates a MAP policy, prompt and consistent action is key. Especially in a world where buying cycles are shrinking, time is of the essence in MAP compliance. Actions your team may take can include issuing warnings, suspending access to products, or putting a hold on shipments or even terminating the retailer's authorization status. It's important to document all actions taken and communicate clearly with the retailer about the reasons for the enforcement measures. As part of this, you may want to think about who on your team is best equipped to handle MAP communications with retailers and consider whether or not there is a conflict of interest or channel conflict.

The Business Benefits of MAP Compliance Impacts Beyond Profit Margin

By preventing price erosion, MAP policies help brands and retailers preserve their profit margins. This is particularly important in competitive markets where price wars can significantly impact profitability. In hundreds of conversations with brands, the idea of price erosion and avoiding the "race to the bottom" has surfaced time and again as one area that is keeping executives up at night. Brands often have a hard time quantifying the direct impact of MAP violations on their business, but by looking at metrics like NPS, Buy Box loss, reduced purchase orders and wholesale SKU velocity, brands can start to understand the financial impact that MAP violations are having on the business. Building Consumer Trust Consistent pricing helps build consumer trust. When customers see stable prices across different retailers, they are more likely to perceive the brand as reliable and trustworthy. This can lead to increased customer loyalty and repeat purchases. That's why understanding how your MAP compliance impacts customer perception is key. Looking at your MAP compliance and examining the correlation between Net Promoter Scores, marketplace reviews and performing sentiment analysis is key to keeping customers and growing the brand. Supporting Brand Positioning MAP compliance supports a brand's positioning in the market. By maintaining a premium price point, brands can differentiate themselves from lower-cost competitors and reinforce their value proposition to consumers. If you have a MAP policy in place or are looking to establish one, it's very likely that your organization has collectively decided that you're not looking to win by being the cheapest option for customers. Going back to this brand value as a guiding principle will make your conversations easier both internally and with retail customers. It's not going to be easy, but it will make for a stronger brand over time. Challenges in MAP Compliance Monitoring and Enforcement - Choosing the Right Provider One of the biggest challenges in MAP compliance is monitoring and enforcement. With the proliferation of online retail channels, it can be difficult for brands to track advertised prices across all platforms. Selecting an automated monitoring and enforcement solution from an established and reputable provider can help, but not all providers are created equal. You'll have to determine what level of solution you are looking for and there are a variety of flavors out there. Do you want monitoring only? Do you require monitoring coupled with a tool that provides a communication workflow? Do you want all of that plus AI generated insights? Because all providers have different approaches and product offerings, you'll have to determine what works for you, based on where you are today and where you want to go. Legal Risks While MAP policies are generally legal, they can sometimes attract legal scrutiny. Brands must ensure that their policies comply with antitrust laws and do not constitute price-fixing. Consulting with legal experts is essential to mitigate these risks. If you have a internal legal team within your company, that's a great place to start. Alternatively, there are legal solutions out there that offer a wide variety of approaches, from policy drafting and ad hoc consultation to fully managed services that will enforce on behalf of your team and do all the heavy lifting for you. Retailer Pushback Retailers may resist MAP policies, especially if they feel that the policies limit their ability to compete on price. This typically happens when brand enforcement efforts are sporadic, inconsistent or even limited by design. If a retailer feels your enforcement efforts are lopsided or that one retailer is receiving preferential treatment, they will start to ignore your communications, reduce their purchase orders or stop carrying your brand entirely. Brands can work to avoid this by communicating the benefits of MAP compliance clearly, establish clear guidelines that apply to all retailers and work collaboratively with retailers to address their concerns as they arise. Conclusion MAP compliance is a crucial strategy for brands looking to protect their value, ensure fair competition, and build strong retailer relationships. By clearly defining MAP policies, educating retail partners, and consistently monitoring and enforcing compliance, brands can effectively manage their pricing strategies and maintain their market positioning. While challenges exist, the benefits of a well-executed MAP program far outweigh the risks of not having one and make it a worthwhile investment for any brand committed to long-term success.

excerpt: El canal online sigue ganando presencia y transformando la manera en que los consumidores realizan sus compras e interactúan con las marcas. De hecho, se proyecta que el comercio...

rawText:
El canal online sigue ganando presencia y transformando la manera en que los consumidores realizan sus compras e interactúan con las marcas. De hecho, se proyecta que el comercio electrónico en Chile crecerá con una tasa de crecimiento anual compuesto (CAGR) del 18% entre 2023 y 2026, hasta llegar a los US$12 mil millones para 2026.

El constante avance del comercio electrónico en las preferencias de los compradores destaca la necesidad que tienen las empresas de optimizar su presencia digital para mantenerse vigentes en un mercado cada vez más competitivo.

A medida que las personas se inclinan cada vez más por realizar compras en línea, la presencia y visibilidad de los productos en las páginas web de retail se vuelve sumamente relevante para las compañías, debido a la influencia que puede tener en las tasas de conversión y de satisfacción de los clientes.

Y si bien, la disponibilidad de los productos puede parecer lo más importante, lo cierto es que un contenido atractivo, elaborado mediante una profunda comprensión del comportamiento del potencial comprador, y que por ende, cumpla con sus expectativas, puede impulsar de manera positiva la conversión.

La importancia de optimizar el contenido

Aunque el aumento de los compradores en línea puede interpretarse como una noticia positiva, la verdad es que también trae consigo una serie de desafíos relacionados con el impacto que tiene el contenido de los productos en los consumidores y la manera en la que ellos interactúan.

Este análisis es lo que permite a las empresas mantenerse competitivas, asegurando que el contenido ofrecido no solo sea capaz de llamar la atención de los consumidores, sino que también se convierta en un catalizador de la acción, como la adición al carrito o la compra final.

Por otro lado, los consumidores en la actualidad son más exigentes que nunca y antes de tomar una decisión de compra, esperan encontrar la mayor cantidad de información posible que les permita tomar la mejor decisión.

Y en caso de no hallar lo que están buscando, lo más probable es que opten por otra marca que sí les dio contenido detallado y preciso del producto.

En ese punto de inflexión, es que un contenido dinámico, mejorado y emocional puede marcar la diferencia.

De hecho, la utilización de plataformas como Omnitok, que ayudan a la gestión de contenido dinámico y emocional, pueden generar un aumento de hasta un 80% en la tasa de conversión, dependiendo de la industria.

Desafíos para las marcas en la gestión del contenido

Sin duda, lograr que las marcas logren entregar contenido que conecte con los consumidores es uno de los grandes retos que enfrentan en la actualidad.

Tomando en cuenta además que no poseen solo un producto, sino que pueden ser cientos o miles, pertenecientes a distintas categorías, lo que complejiza en gran medida la tarea de mantener el contenido actualizado frente a los cambios constantes en las especificaciones de los productos.

A este desafío se le suma que la gestión de la información de productos en las plataformas de retail es realizada principalmente por los minoristas, lo que coarta en gran medida las posibilidades de que las empresas logren transmitir el potencial de sus productos de manera convincente, por lo que no logran destacar, disminuyendo las posibilidades de cautivar a los potenciales consumidores.

Y si a eso se le añade que la actualización de la información de los productos en los sitios tiene que hacerse de forma manual, yendo de retail en retail, la inversión de tiempo y recursos es bastante alta, ralentizando estos procesos y volviéndolos susceptibles a errores en la presentación del contenido.

Si las empresas no logran resolver estos desafíos de manera adecuada, la reputación de la marca puede verse afectada de manera negativa, al poner en entredicho su capacidad de satisfacer la necesidad de información precisa y actualizada de los consumidores.

Optimiza el contenido de tu marca con Omnitok

Para sortear estos desafíos, l as empresas optan por asociarse con soluciones tecnológicas como Omnitok, que garantiza la precisión y la actualización constante de los datos, por lo que no solo reduce los problemas operativos, sino que además entrega contenido emocional y dinámico a los consumidores, facilitando su interacción con la información de los productos, logrando una experiencia de compra más significativa y sin fricciones, aumentando las posibilidades de conversión.

Omnitok ofrece una serie de innovaciones para optimizar el contenido, como la integración de fichas de contenido enriquecido, que permite a las marcas controlar y mejorar la presentación de sus productos en los sitios web minoristas, sincronizando y distribuyendo la información de manera automática, garantizando que la información esté actualizada cada uno de ellos y liberando a los equipos de la gestión manual de cada plataforma de venta.

Por otra parte, estas fichas de contenido enriquecido permiten la generación de contenido dinámico y emocional, para que las marcas logren generar un vínculo más profundo con el cliente, al resonar con sus necesidades y deseos, para influir de esta manera en las decisiones de compra.

El uso de Omnitok empodera a las marcas, entregándoles el control sobre cómo se comunica su producto, con las ventajas que supone la automatización de ciertos procesos, como la prevención de errores o retrasos en la generación de contenido, asegurando que cada imagen y palabra refleje de manera íntegra el mensaje que quieren transmitir.

Con más de una década de experiencia en el mercado, estamos comprometidos a ser tu partner tecnológico, ayudándote a maximizar la optimización del contenido en los sitios minoristas.

title: El futuro del ecommerce: tecnología y experiencia del cliente
seoTitle: El futuro del ecommerce: tecnología y experiencia del cliente | Omnitok
metaDescription: Explora cómo tecnología, contenido y experiencia del cliente están redefiniendo el futuro del ecommerce para las marcas.
h1: El futuro del ecommerce: tecnología y experiencia del cliente
excerpt: Sin lugar a dudas, el comercio electrónico ha revolucionado la manera en que las personas compran y cómo las empresas venden, convirtiéndose en un pilar integral de la economía mundial.
coverImageAlt: Ilustración sobre futuro del ecommerce para marcas que venden en retailers y marketplaces

relatedLinks.description: Monitorea ejecucion digital, contenido, precios y disponibilidad para detectar fricciones que impactan la conversion.

rawText:
Sin lugar a dudas, el comercio electrónico ha revolucionado la manera en que las personas compran y cómo las empresas venden, convirtiéndose en un pilar integral de la economía mundial.

De hecho, durante la pandemia, el rol que cumplió el e-commerce fue vital para aminorar los efectos que trajo la paralización de la actividad presencial.

Todo indica que, a pesar del retorno a la normalidad, muchos seguirán privilegiando hacer sus compras en línea y se espera que represente el 25% de las ventas minoristas para el año que viene.

En ese sentido, los desafíos que enfrentan aquellas marcas que trabajan en el comercio digital tienen gran relación con los avances tecnológicos, y cómo la competencia se ha vuelto cada vez más estrecha a medida que más compañías van incorporando la innovación en sus equipos.

Frente a esto, surge la necesidad de marcar la diferencia y el concepto de "experiencia del cliente" cobra fuerza debido a que se comprobó lo importante que resulta para los consumidores no solo encontrar productos de calidad a un precio razonable, sino que la experiencia de compra sea agradable y sin fricciones.

En este artículo exploraremos cómo estas dos fuerzas están transformando el panorama del comercio digital, y cómo las empresas que habitan en este ecosistema pueden impulsar su crecimiento usando herramientas y soluciones que les permitan entregar experiencias más personalizadas, seguras y eficientes a sus potenciales compradores. ¿

Cómo ha evolucionado el comercio digital?

El e-commerce ha experimentado una radical transformación desde sus inicios en la década de los 90.

Lo que empezó como simples sitios web para vender productos han ido evolucionando de la mano de los avances tecnológicos hacia plataformas cada vez más sofisticadas, cuyo foco en la actualidad está en crear una experiencia de compra excepcional que cautive a los clientes.

La evolución de este sector ha sido casi a la par que la transformación del consumidor, el que exige con mayor frecuencia un trato hiperpersonalizado. Por lo que, entender y adaptarse al comportamiento del shopper, entregándole una experiencia que responda a sus requerimientos, es lo que actualmente representa el éxito en las ventas.

El uso de Inteligencia Artificial (IA) ha permitido que las empresas alcancen niveles de personalización nunca antes vistos, mediante la aplicación de algoritmos de aprendizaje automático, las marcas pueden analizar el comportamiento del usuario para así ofrecer recomendaciones que se ajusten de forma precisa a sus necesidades, aumentando la posibilidad de compra.

Esta tecnología se ve reforzada por la implementación del Big Data, en donde las compañías pueden gestionar y analizar grandes volúmenes de datos -que pueden ser obtenido en cada uno de los puntos de contacto del viaje del cliente- para anticipar tendencias y comportamientos del consumidor, para optimizar estrategias de marketing y ventas.

Grandes compañías han logrado un éxito notable gracias a estas innovaciones. Uno de los casos más conocidos es Amazon, que ha utilizado la inteligencia artificial de forma estratégica, consiguiendo aumentar sus ventas.

Su algoritmo de recomendaciones, que se basa en el comportamiento de navegación y compra de los usuarios, ha sido extremadamente efectivo a la hora de crear una experiencia de compra totalmente personalizada. ¿

Por qué la experiencia del cliente importa en el e-commerce?

La experiencia del cliente (o CX por sus siglas en inglés) alude a la percepción global que tienen las personas sobre su interacción con una empresa o servicio a lo largo de todo su viaje de compra, desde el primer contacto hasta el servicio post venta y tiene un impacto directo en la satisfacción, aumentando la lealtad y oportunidades.

¿Y por qué es tan importante? Una experiencia positiva puede marcar la diferencia respecto a los competidores.

En un mercado altamente saturado, en donde los precios y productos no tienen mucha diferencia, entregar una experiencia de compra fluida, empática y agradable puede influir en la atracción y retención de clientes, aumentando las conversiones y reduciendo el abandono del carrito de compras.

Pero eso no es lo único. La personalización es otro elemento muy relevante a la hora de generar una experiencia excepcional: las personas esperan que las marcas estén conscientes de sus necesidades y les ofrezcan productos y/o servicios que estén en concordancia con ellas.

Además, la marca debe ser lo suficientemente rápida en sus procesos, ya que el tiempo es un recurso muy valorado en la actualidad, y en consecuencia, los shoppers prefieren que las experiencias sean ágiles y eficientes, esperándose lo mismo para la atención al consumidor, en donde responder pronto a las consultas y problemas que tenga el cliente puede transformar una venta perdida en un cliente leal. Omnitok: la perfecta fusión de tecnología y experiencia

El futuro del comercio digital está claramente marcado por la fusión de tecnología y experiencia del cliente.

Aquellas empresas que adopten estas tendencias y utilicen la tecnología para mejorar la experiencia del cliente estarán mejor posicionadas para prosperar en un entorno que día a día se vuelve más competitivo.

Omnitok se enfoca en optimizar esta experiencia al potenciar las páginas de producto en los sitios web del retail con contenido emocional y dinámico, lo que permite a las personas conectar de manera más profunda con la marca, ofreciendo descripciones y contenido audiovisual atractivo, facilitando la toma de decisiones y aumentando la satisfacción del potencial comprador. Además, esta plataforma también ofrece asistencia personalizada, ya sea a través de chat, videollamadas e inteligencia artificial conversacional, asegurándose que los consumidores reciban ayuda inmediata y efectiva, mejorando la percepción de la marca y entregando un servicio fluido y coherente.

Ante todo, y considerando la alta competitividad del mercado actual, lograr diferenciarse por la entrega de una experiencia excepcional es clave. En esa línea, Omnitok no solo ayuda a atraer nuevos clientes, sino que también fomenta la fidelización al asegurar que cada interacción sea positiva y memorable, aumentando el valor de vida del cliente y generando un boca a boca positivo, impulsando así el crecimiento y la reputación de tu marca en el e-commerce.

title: Consumidor digital: cómo entender mejor al visitante de tu marca
seoTitle: Consumidor digital: cómo entender mejor al visitante de tu marca | Omnitok
metaDescription: Conoce las características del consumidor digital y cómo usar ese entendimiento para mejorar contenido, experiencia y conversión.
h1: Consumidor digital: cómo entender mejor al visitante de tu marca
excerpt: Muchos recuerdan con nostalgia la época en la que internet se utilizaba principalmente para buscar información respecto a productos y servicios. Actualmente, la participación más activa...
coverImageAlt: Ilustración sobre consumidor digital en ecommerce para marcas que venden en retailers y marketplaces

rawText:
Muchos recuerdan con nostalgia la época en la que internet se utilizaba principalmente para buscar información respecto a productos y servicios. Actualmente, la participación más activa de los consumidores y un mayor acceso a dispositivos han cambiado el panorama y las reglas del juego para quienes venden en sitios online.

En los últimos años, se ha experimentado un cambio significativo en el comportamiento del comprador online, más allá de los cambios que trajo consigo la pandemia. Según el informe "

Digital 2023: Global Overview Report ", ha habido un aumento en el número de usuarios de internet a nivel mundial, a lo que se suma un declive inesperado en el tiempo promedio de la actividad en línea, lo que ha transformado la forma en que las personas buscan la información, llevando a que las marcas diversifiquen su inversión en publicidad digital.

De ahí que conocer en profundidad a su potencial consumidor digital se ha convertido en un imperativo para las compañías, debido a los enormes beneficios que reporta esta información.

Sin embargo, quienes trabajan en e-commerce coinciden que acceder a ese valioso conocimiento puede resultar mucho más complejo de lo que se lee, debido a los desafíos que implica la natural necesidad de la gente por obtener una atención personalizada.

Y si bien, las necesidades de un shopper pueden ser tan variadas como la cantidad de personas que hay en el mundo, existen algunos puntos que suelen ser comunes para todos los compradores.

En este artículo te daremos algunas pistas que te podrían ayudar a reconocerlo.

¿Quiénes compran por internet?

Una de las principales dudas que surgen cuando se habla de consumidor digital es definir quién compra por internet. Datos como la edad, género o lugar donde vive se transforman en datos cruciales para descubrir cuáles son sus motivaciones o necesidades.

En el caso de nuestro país, según la encuesta "Radiografía del eCommerce en Chile" realizada por Mercado Libre y GfK, reveló que un 49% de los sondeados se reconocen como compradores intensivos por internet. Y que el segmento en donde se agrupan la mayor cantidad de consumidores es el de entre 25 y 34 años y los de 65 años o más.

Otro de los datos interesantes que revela esta investigación es que las principales motivaciones que tienen las personas para comprar en línea son: "porque me acomoda recibir el producto en mi casa o el lugar que yo elija" con un 83%; "compro online porque ahorro tiempo" con un 82%, y "me ayuda a acceder a productos que no hay en las tiendas de donde vivo" con un 80% de preferencias.

¿Qué es lo que piden los consumidores a los sitios online?

Como ya mencionamos, aunque las exigencias de un comprador en línea pueden ser muy variadas, existen algunos tópicos que suelen ser comunes para la gran mayoría. Por ejemplo, la usabilidad y la experiencia de compra en la actualidad son absolutamente vitales para el éxito de una empresa en el comercio digital.

Esta idea es reafirmada por el estudio de Mercado Libre, en donde algunos puntos identificados como relevantes para generar una mejor experiencia tienen relación con "que tengan ofertas y descuentos de productos" (45%), "buen sistema de devolución de productos" (29%), y que haya precisión en el "stock disponible de productos" (29%).

Por su parte, un cliente promedio tiende a demandar información, que tal vez no expresa de manera clara, pero que sí queda en evidencia en sus decisiones de compra, donde no solo abarca al producto o a la marca, sino que también guarda mucha relación con temas relacionados con el sector. En ese sentido, que los sitios cuenten con imágenes, videos y contenido relevante son esenciales para los shoppers, porque aunque puedan prescindir del tacto a la hora de definirse por un artículo, eso no es sinónimo de que no necesiten analizar lo que van a adquirir.

¿Qué características tiene el consumidor online que llega a tu marca?

Gracias a esta información, podemos acercarnos a qué es lo que busca un potencial comprador de tu marca. Sin embargo, para poder ofrecer de manera adecuada necesitas muchísima más información. A continuación, buscaremos profundizar qué otras características posee el shopper que llega a tus productos. Es proactivo: el perfil de los consumidores online suele ser el de un individuo que no espera que le llegue todo hecho, sino que busca, se informa, pero por sobre todo, compara antes de realizar una transacción. Por esta razón, es vital que la marca logre destacar en distintos aspectos, tanto a la hora de conquistar nuevos clientes como al momento de fidelizar a quienes ya han comprado, ofreciéndoles justo lo que están buscando. Es digital: casi resulta redundante mencionarlo, pero nunca está de más recordar que es un usuario acostumbrado a los entornos en línea y que posee conocimientos digitales. Al respecto, no sientas temor de automatizar todos los procesos posibles, ya que le dará confianza y hará que se sienta mucho más cómodo interactuando con tu marca. Es exigente: esta característica suele ser una de las más complicadas de abordar. Pero, la razón de esta conducta es muy sencilla: existen miles de opciones de productos en internet, lo que genera una tendencia a que se incline por aquello que considera una mejor alternativa. El reto para ti es convencerlo de que tus productos son la mejor opción.

Vive en un aprendizaje constante: debido al contexto histórico y social en el que está inmerso, este consumidor vive constantemente aprendiendo y adquiriendo nuevas experiencias, lo que obliga a los líderes de las empresas a seguir su ritmo para poder dar respuesta a sus requerimientos. Es polivalente: la mayor parte de los consumidores poseen un perfil polivalente, aplicándolo a todos los ámbitos de su vida, tanto en las cuestiones laborales como en las formativas. Esto les otorga una capacidad atencional y selectiva que se traduce en una nueva exigencia para los comercios online.

title: IA para retención de clientes: casos de uso y oportunidades reales
seoTitle: IA para retención de clientes: casos de uso y oportunidades reales | Omnitok
metaDescription: Descubre cómo usar inteligencia artificial para mejorar retención de clientes, personalización y experiencia de compra en ecommerce.

excerpt: Debido a los grandes avances en la tecnología, la competencia en el mercado digital se vuelve feroz en cuestión de segundos. De ahí que la retención de clientes se haya transformado en...

rawText:
Debido a los grandes avances en la tecnología, la competencia en el mercado digital se vuelve feroz en cuestión de segundos. De ahí que la retención de clientes se haya transformado en una necesidad importante para las empresas que buscan consolidar su crecimiento y la lealtad de sus consumidores.

Es un hecho que retener y mantener satisfechos a los clientes existentes es indiscutiblemente más rentable que la adquisición de nuevos. Sin embargo, aunque sea más barato, no implica que esté exento de desafíos.

Basta solo una mala experiencia para que un cliente "leal" decida buscar alternativas con la competencia.

Por lo mismo, hay que tener un especial cuidado para que las personas que compran sigan haciéndolo. Afortunadamente, con la ayuda de la inteligencia artificial, esta tarea se ha vuelto mucho más sencilla, debido al potencial que posee esta tecnología para revolucionar la forma en que las empresas interactúan con sus clientes, que va desde la automatización hasta el análisis predictivo.

Al respecto, investigaciones de McKinsey señalan que aquellas compañías que utilizan la inteligencia artificial en el servicio al cliente pueden aumentar su retención hasta en un 10%, reduciendo además entre un 20% a un 40% los costos operativos de esta área.

Y si bien, existen muchas herramientas que ayudan a la retención de clientes, lo cierto es que no funcionan solas y que hacen necesario contar con una buena estrategia para que impulsar el crecimiento de tu empresa, tanto con personas nuevas, pero también con aquellos clientes fieles.

En este artículo profundizaremos cómo puedes mejorar tu estrategia de la mano de la inteligencia artificial. ¿

Por qué es importante la retención de clientes?

Una buena estrategia de retención consiste sobre todo, en ganarse la confianza de las personas.

Para conseguirlo, es imperativo generar una comunicación transparente y fluida, ya que gracias a eso, puedes obtener una valiosa retroalimentación respecto a cómo mejorar tu producto o servicio.

Aquellos clientes que se mantienen fieles a tu compañía pueden ayudarte a perfeccionar los productos existentes, cómo desarrollar nuevos o mejorar la atención al cliente; tres pilares fundamentales para mantener la competitividad en el mercado.

Por otra parte, los clientes leales son más propensos a incrementar su gasto a lo largo del tiempo, no solo porque siguen comprando, sino que además pueden estar dispuestos a pagar precios más altos por productos o servicios adicionales, oportunidades que puedes aprovechar a través de la implementación de estrategias como el cross-selling o el up-selling.

Pero eso no es todo. Porque un consumidor satisfecho es una de las mejores herramientas de marketing que una empresa puede tener.

Un cliente feliz no solo regresa para comprar más, sino que no tendrá problemas en promover tus productos con su familia, amigos o redes sociales, lo que puede generar nuevos clientes para tu marca de forma orgánica y sin comprometer costos adicionales.

Además del beneficio económico, la retención de clientes también trae consigo otras ventajas, como la construcción de relaciones significativas con las personas que te han comprado.

Cuando inviertes en este ítem, le demuestras a tus consumidores que los valoras y que estás dispuesto a seguir entregándoles valor a largo plazo, fortaleciendo su lealtad y marcando la diferencia con respecto a la competencia.

Cómo retener clientes con la ayuda de la IA?

La verdad sea dicha: sin un buen servicio al cliente no es posible conseguir la retención.

Después de todo, un consumidor que recibió una experiencia grata y personalizada, ya sea a través de la resolución de dudas o entrega de recomendaciones, es mucho más probable que regrese.

Por lo mismo, para impulsar una estrategia de retención efectiva para tu negocio, es vital que identifiques todos los puntos de contacto en un recorrido de compra.

En ellos, las personas deberán recibir un servicio excepcional y para eso, puedes apoyarte con herramientas o soluciones de inteligencia artificial. Chatbots: contar con un asistente virtual generado por inteligencia artificial puede ser sumamente útil para entregar información sobre tus productos, ya sea respondiendo las dudas que pueda tener el potencial comprador o recomendando la mejor opción que se adapte a sus necesidades. Ofertas personalizadas: otra estrategia que puede ser muy exitosa consiste en ofrecer alguna oferta o promoción personalizada a aquellas personas que ya han comprado tus productos.

En estos casos, una herramienta de IA que te permita acceder a la data de esos clientes para realizar análisis predictivos, mediante la integración de técnicas como el modelado, machine learning, estadísticas, entre otros, puede ayudarte a que sea más precisa. Contenido emocional: apelar a las emociones de los consumidores a través de historias, imágenes, videos, música e incluso la propia experiencia de compra es una estrategia sumamente efectiva.

Cuando se logra crear contenido emocionalmente resonante, la marca establece una conexión poderosa con los posibles clientes, lo que no solo fomenta una mayor lealtad y compromiso, sino que también impulsa las ventas.

Omnitok: aliado tecnológico que impulsa la retención de clientes

Considerando que el entorno digital es cada vez más competitivo, se hace necesario contar con herramientas tecnológicas que mejoren la interacción con los clientes, y de esta forma potenciar las estrategias de retención.

Omnitok es una de las mejores soluciones existentes en el mercado, al transformar la experiencia de compra con contenido emocional efectivo y entregando asistencia virtual personalizada en todo el recorrido de compra en un sitio web de retail.

Gracias a esto, nuestros clientes pueden destacar frente a sus competidores, experimentando un significativo aumento de las ventas.

Las cifras son contundentes: Omnitok mejora en un 80% las tasas de conversión, incrementa en un 50% la productividad y logra una agilidad del 100% en lanzamientos de mercado en tiempo real.

Con más de una década de experiencia en el mercado, estamos comprometidos a ser tu partner tecnológico, ayudándote a maximizar tu estrategia de retención de clientes en las páginas web de retail.

title: Psicología de compra en ecommerce: qué factores influyen más
seoTitle: Psicología de compra en ecommerce: qué factores influyen más | Omnitok
metaDescription: Analiza los factores de psicología de compra que influyen en ecommerce y cómo aplicarlos sin sacrificar claridad ni experiencia.
h1: Psicología de compra en ecommerce: qué factores influyen más
excerpt: "Siempre me traiciona la razón. Y me domina el corazón". Esta frase de Camilo Sesto retrata de manera exacta la manera en que las personas se comportan al momento de realizar una compra....
